coredata.dc:title: SnO2 coated carbon nanospheres: Facile preparation and electrochemical performances as anode materials for lithium ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of National Sleep Foundation.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of National Sleep Foundation.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yujin
Tang, Gao
Shi, Lei
Zeng, Yingyi
Zhou, Yongheng
Tao, Yushan
Bai, Liqun
Luo, Qiuyue
Li, Dan
Song, Wenlong
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the SnO2/C nanocomposites as the anode material for lithium ion batteries (LIBs) were successfully prepared by ultrasonic dispersing of the hydrothermally-synthesized carbon nanospheres in SnCl2 solution and aging for a period of time and then calcination of the dried intermediates in an inert atmosphere. As demonstrated by the experimental results, the obtained SnO2/C nanocomposites were composed of SnO2 nanocrystals (∼10 nm) coated uniformly on the surface of amorphous carbon nanospheres with diameters ranging from 200 nm to 240 nm. The electrochemical test results show that the prepared SnO2/C nanocomposites exhibited higher reversible capacity than the carbon nanospheres, and better cycling performance than the SnO2 nanocrystals. The enhanced electrochemical performance of SnO2/C nanocomposites could be due to the fact that the coupling of SnO2 nanocrystals and carbon nanospheres could not only effectively improve the mobility of charge carriers, but also effectively inhibit the collapse of the SnO2 structure at the expense of a part of the capacity, thus resulting in better structural stability of the SnO2/C nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SnO2/C nanocomposites
Anode materials
Lithium ion batteries
Electrochemical performances

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of (A) C nanospheres, (B) SnO2 nanocrystals and (C) SnO2/C nanocomposites.
FESEM images of (A) C nanospheres, (B) SnO2 nanocrystals, and (C) SnO2/C nanocomposites; (D) EDS of the SnO2/C nanocomposites.

The CV curves of the prepared (A) carbon nanospheres, (B) SnO2 nanocrystals and (C) SnO2/C nanocomposites anodes for the first two cycles measured at room temperature between 0 and 3.0 V (vs. Li/Li+) at a scan rate of 0.05 mV s−1.
The galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of the cells with the carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals, and SnO2/C nanocomposites anodes for the first cycle.
The cycle performance (solid dots) and coulombic efficiency (hollow dots) of the C nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites at the constant current of 100 mA/g.

head.ce:title: SnO2 coated carbon nanospheres: Facile preparation and electrochemical performances as anode materials for lithium ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the SnO2/C nanocomposites as the anode material for lithium ion batteries (LIBs) were successfully prepared by ultrasonic dispersing of the hydrothermally-synthesized carbon nanospheres in SnCl2 solution and aging for a period of time and then calcination of the dried intermediates in an inert atmosphere. As demonstrated by the experimental results, the obtained SnO2/C nanocomposites were composed of SnO2 nanocrystals (∼10 nm) coated uniformly on the surface of amorphous carbon nanospheres with diameters ranging from 200 nm to 240 nm. The electrochemical test results show that the prepared SnO2/C nanocomposites exhibited higher reversible capacity than the carbon nanospheres, and better cycling performance than the SnO2 nanocrystals. The enhanced electrochemical performance of SnO2/C nanocomposites could be due to the fact that the coupling of SnO2 nanocrystals and carbon nanospheres could not only effectively improve the mobility of charge carriers, but also effectively inhibit the collapse of the SnO2 structure at the expense of a part of the capacity, thus resulting in better structural stability of the SnO2/C nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have been successfully applied in many fields such as portable electronic devices and new energy vehicles due to their advantages of high specific energy, long cycle life, no memory effect, wide operating temperature range and environmental friendliness, and have attracted more and more attention in recent years. As known, anode material is an important part of LIB, which has an important influence on the LIB's electrochemical lithium storage performance. At present, graphite is the main anode material for commercial LIBs, but its low capacity (372 mAh g−1) cannot meet the development demand of high energy density LIBs. SnO2 has a high theoretical capacity of 1494 mAh g−1 and a suitable lithiation potential (1.0 V vs. Li/Li+) as a metal oxide anode material for LIBs [1]. In addition, SnO2 is relatively abundant in nature, cheap and easy to prepare, which is expected to be a promising anode material for the next generation of high energy and high power LIBs. However, there is an irreversible reaction in the lithiation process of SnO2, thus resulting in a large initial capacity loss. Moreover, SnO2 has poor electrical conductivity and volume expansion during the charging and discharging process, which leads to the powder agglomeration and poor cycle stability [2,3]. Therefore, many efforts have been made to solve the above problems of SnO2 anode materials [4], such as nanostructural design [5,6], element doping [7,8], and construction of composite with other materials [9,10]. Among them, the construction of SnO2-based composite anode materials has been proved to be an effective approach for improving the electrochemical lithium storage performance of SnO2 [11].

Due to their high conductivities and various forms, carbon materials have been widely used to composite with other anode materials to fabricate the carbon-based composite anode materials, which can improve the conductivity of anode materials and alleviate their volume change during the charging and discharging process [12]. In particular, carbon nanospheres, as a kind of carbon materials, possess excellent structural stability and low surface-volume ratio, as well as a short diffusion path of lithium ions. Therefore, carbon nanospheres, as anode materials for LIBs, exhibit excellent lithium ion diffusion, electrical conductivity and structural stability, but low theoretical specific capacity is the key factor restricting their development [13]. In view of the advantages and disadvantages of SnO2 and carbon nanospheres as anode materials for LIBs mentioned above, this work attempts to load SnO2 nanocrystals onto the surface of carbon nanospheres to construct SnO2/C composite anode materials, in order to obtain higher capacity and more stable electrochemical lithium storage performance.
The preparation of SnO2/C nanocomposites could be divided into two steps: hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanospheres and the coating of SnO2 nanocrystals onto the surface of carbon nanospheres.

The preparation process of carbon nanospheres was as follows. Firstly, 6 g of glucose was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, and the obtained solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. Then the autoclave was sealed and reacted at 180 ℃ for 7 h. After the reaction, the resulting purplish-brown carbon nanospheres were separated by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol for several times, and finally dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

Preparation of SnO2/C nanocomposites: (i) 0.2 g of the prepared carbon nanospheres were ultrasonically dispersed in 35 mL of absolute ethanol, and then 3.94 g of SnCl2·2 H2O was added to the dispersion solution, followed by ultrasonic for 1.5 h. (ii) The solution obtained above was aged at room temperature for 5 h, then rinsed with absolute ethanol and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. (iii) The dried intermediate powders were ground evenly and put into a crucible, and then put into a tube furnace. The final product of SnO2/C nanocomposites was obtained by calcination at 500 ℃ in argon atmosphere for 2 h. For comparison, pure SnO2 nanocrystals were obtained by calcination of SnO2/C nanocomposites at 500 ℃ for 2 h in air.

The crystal structures of the obtained samples were examined by using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Thermo Scientific ARL X'TRA), where the X-ray source was Cu Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å) and the scanning range was 10°–80°. The morphological microstructures and elemental analysis of the products were obtained by using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi-SU8010, Japan) combined with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS).

The prepared anode materials were assembled into CR2025 coin cells, and then a series of electrochemical tests were performed, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), charge and discharge curves, and cyclic performance. The anodes were fabricated by casting the slurry consisting of 80 wt% of active material (the prepared carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals, or SnO2/C nanocomposites), 10 wt% of acetylene black as a conductive material, and 10 wt% of poly(vinylidenefluoride) (PVDF) binder in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) onto a copper foil. The fabricated electrode sheets were then dried at 120 °C under vacuum overnight and roll pressed, and further punched into circular discs for coin cells. The coin cell (CR2025) was fabricated using the circular disc as the active electrode and lithium metal as the counter electrode separated by the polypropylene separator (Celgard 2400). The electrolyte used in the cells was a solution of 1 M LiPF6 in a mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1 by volume). These cells were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox (Super 1220/750, MIKROUNA) and galvanostatically cycled between 0 V and 1.5 V (vs. Li/Li+) on a multi-channel battery cycler (Land Battery Test System). The cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurements were carried out at room temperature on the same electrochemical workstation over the potential range 0–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+ at the scan rate of 0.05 mV s−1.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the prepared carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites. As shown, the diffraction peaks of the obtained carbon nanospheres prepared by hydrothermal method were broad, showing typical amorphous carbon structure [14]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of the prepared SnO2 nanocrystals and the SnO2/C nanocomposites were consistent with the tetragonal rutile structure of SnO2 (JCPDS card no. 41-1445) [15]. In comparison with that of SnO2, the XRD curve of the SnO2/C nanocomposites had no obvious changes except the intensities of diffraction peak decreased to a certain extent, which indicates that the presence or absence of carbon microspheres did not affect the crystal structure of SnO2, and also indicates that the SnO2 coating layer of the prepared SnO2/C nanocomposites had better crystallinity.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images of the prepared carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites, as well as the EDS result of the SnO2/C nanospheres. As shown, the carbon nanospheres synthesized by hydrothermal method exhibited a regular spherical morphology with a particle size range of 200–240 nm (Fig. 2A), and some carbon nanospheres were linked together, which could be attributed to the cross-linking reaction occurring during the hydrothermal process [16]. The SnO2 nanocrystals prepared by air calcination of the SnO2/C nanocomposites (Fig. 2B) exhibited a granular structure with a uniform particle size of about 10 nm, and there was a certain amount of agglomeration among the particles. For the obtained SnO2/C nanocomposites (Fig. 2C), it can be seen that the particles could still maintain a relatively regular spherical structure with a particle size of about 250–280 nm, which was slightly larger than that of carbon nanospheres, probably because their surface was covered with a layer of tiny nanocrystals (∼10 nm). The EDS results of the SnO2/C nanocomposites (Fig. 2D) confirm that the nanocomposites were mainly composed of three elements, i.e., C, Sn and O. Based on the FESEM morphology and XRD data, it can be inferred that many small SnO2 nanocrystals were uniformly coated on the surface of carbon nanospheres, thus forming the SnO2/C nanocomposites.

The electrochemical properties of the prepared different samples were examined by a variety of electrochemical methods, including CV curves, galvanostatic charge-discharge curves, rate performances and electrochemical impedance spectra. Fig. 3 shows the CV curves of the prepared carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites for the first two cycles. For carbon nanospheres (Fig. 3A), a cathodic reduction peak appeared at around 0.85 V in the first cycle, which could be attributed to the reaction between carbon nanospheres and Li ions, while the lithiation reaction mainly occurred below 0.5 V, corresponding to two cathodic reduction peaks at around 0.4 V and 0.2 V. In particular, the characteristic peak at about 0.4 V disappeared during the second cycle, indicating that this process was also accompanied by the formation of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [17,18]. In the anodic process, the delithiation reaction mainly occurred at the two broad peaks of 0.5 V and 1.25 V [19]. For the prepared SnO2 anode (Fig. 3B), two major reduction peaks at 0.75 V and 0.2 V appeared in the first cycle. The former could be attributed to the reaction between SnO2 and Li+ to form Sn and Li2O, as shown in Equation (1):
4
Li
+
+ Sn
O
2
+ 4
e
−
→ 2
Li
2
O + Sn
(1), accompanied by the formation of SEI film. The latter could be attributed to the formation of Li-Sn alloy (LiySn), as described in Equation (2) below:
x
Li
+
+ Sn + x
e
−
→
Li
x
Sn
(2) [20]. In the anodic process, two oxidation peaks appeared at about 0.5 V and 1.25 V, respectively, which were the reverse alloying reaction of Li-Sn alloy and the decomposition reaction of Li2O [21]. For the prepared SnO2/C nanocomposites anode (Fig. 3C), three cathodic reduction peaks appeared at around 0.9 V, 0.5 V and 0.2 V in the first cycle, which could be attributed to the formation of SEI film, the lithiation reaction of SnO2 reduction to Sn and Li2O, and the further reduction of Sn to LixSn alloy. In contrast, there appeared two oxidation peaks at around 0.5 V and 1.25 V in the anodic oxidation process, corresponding to the process of Sn formation from the LixSn alloy delithiation and the gradual decomposition of Li2O, as well as the process of Li+ removal from carbon nanospheres [22,23].

Fig. 4 shows the first galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites. The first charge/discharge capacities of carbon nanospheres were 255 mAh g−1 and 848.8 mAh g−1, respectively, and the first coulomb efficiency (CE) was 30 %. The main reason for the huge irreversible capacity loss could be due to the fact that the electrolyte was reduced and decomposed on the electrode surface to form SEI film [23,24]. In addition, the irreversible reaction occurred between lithium ion and impurities adsorbed by carbon nanospheres [25]. The first charge/discharge capacities of SnO2 were 669.2 mAh g−1 and 1476 mAh g−1, respectively, and the first CE was 45.3 %. The irreversible capacity loss of 54.7 % was probably due to the formation of Sn and Li2O by the reaction between SnO2 and Li ions, and the formation of SEI film on the surface of the electrode [26,27]. For the SnO2/C nanocomposites, the first charge/discharge capacities were 405.5 mAh g−1 and 1207 mAh g−1, respectively. The first CE of SnO2/C nanocomposites was 33.6 %, which was higher than that of carbon nanospheres and lower than that of SnO2. It can be seen that compared with SnO2, the capacity of the first charge and discharge of SnO2/C nanocomposites was sacrificed, and the first CE was also not improved. Similar phenomena have been observed in previously-reported literature [28,29].

Fig. 5 shows the charge-discharge cycle performances and Coulomb efficiencies for the first 50 cycles of the carbon nanospheres, SnO2 nanocrystals and SnO2/C nanocomposites as an anode material for LIBs at a current density of 100 mA/g in the potential window of 0–1.5 V (vs. Li/Li+). It can be observed that the first reversible discharge specific capacity of carbon nanospheres was 330 mAh g−1, and the reversible discharge capacity retained 22.6 % after 50 cycles. The first reversible discharge capacity of SnO2 nanocrystals was 739.2 mAh g−1, and only 2 % of reversible discharge capacity was retained after 50 cycles, further confirming the very poor cycling performance of SnO2 nanomaterials as previously reported [26,30,31]. The initial reversible discharge capacity of SnO2/C nanocomposites was 555.5 mAh g−1, and the reversible discharge capacity retained 29 % after 50 cycles. The cyclic stability of the nanocomposites was greatly improved. Compared with the carbon nanospheres and SnO2 nanocrystals, the SnO2/C nanocomposites exhibited more stable cycling performance and higher reversible capacity, which combined the advantages of the stability of carbon nanospheres and the high capacity of SnO2 nanocrystals. This phenomenon has also been observed in other reported work on SnO2/C nanocomposites as anodes for LIBs [32,33]. It can be considered that the SnO2/C nanocomposites sacrificed their capacity to some extent in exchange for their cyclic stability during the charging and discharging process. In addition, the CE of the SnO2/C nanocomposites was also significantly improved compared with that of SnO2, reaching 87.5 % in the third cycle. In our view, the SnO2 nanocrystals coated on the surface of the carbon nanospheres had enough space between the SnO2 nanocrystals to cope with the volume stress caused by the intercalation and deintercalation of Li ions, thus resulting in much better electrochemical lithium ion storage performance of the SnO2/C nanocomposites.

In this work, the SnO2/C nanocomposites were prepared by impregnating the hydrothermally-synthesized carbon nanospheres in the SnO2 precursor solution and then calcining the dried intermediates under an inert atmosphere at high temperature. The structural and morphological features of the obtained samples were characterized and analyzed by means of SEM, XRD and EDS, and their electrochemical lithium ion storage performance was explored by means of electrochemical measurements. The experimental results show that the SnO2/C nanocomposites were composed of crystalline SnO2 nanocrystals with a size of about 10 nm coated on the surface of amorphous carbon nanospheres with a size of about 200 nm. Compared with SnO2 nanocrystals and carbon nanospheres, the SnO2/C nanocomposites exhibited superior electrochemical lithium ion storage performance, with much better charging-discharge cycling stability and higher reversible capacity. Apparently, the SnO2/C nanocomposites took into account the advantages of both SnO2 nanocrystals and C nanospheres as anode materials for lithium ion batteries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the College Student's Science and Technology Innovation Activity Plan of Zhejiang Province (Xinmiao Talent Project) (No. 2021R409015), and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LQ20F040007, LQ19F040004).

Mater. 29, 1605006. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201605006
